title: The 1967 Radioactive ATM
desc:
27 June 1967: Barclays switched on the world's first cash machine in Enfield, London — beating Sweden's rival by just 9 days. No plastic cards existed. You bought single-use paper vouchers laced with mildly radioactive carbon-14, typed a PIN, and got at most £10 (about £159 today) — enough for a wild weekend. The inventor's safety pitch: you'd have to EAT 136,000 vouchers to feel anything. His wife could only remember 4 digits, so 4-digit PINs became the world standard. He never patented it — and the card-and-PIN system in every modern ATM was actually patented by James Goodfellow in May 1966, 13 months earlier.

#BrokeToBuilt #history #ATM #invention
